articles.title: Write code like a human will maintain it
articles.summary: Posts can be written in a way that mimics how humans would write code, with attention to best practices and readability. One of the best things about Large Language Models is that they'll write code for you, all day long, freeing up time for more important tasks. But as I've noticed on my project, letting LLMs handle maintenance can lead to a slippery slope where they start writing their own code based on your previous work. This means that every shortcut or merge into the codebase becomes a signal about how things are done here, and it's harder to catch errors later. Eventually, you'll need to roll up your sleeves and manually fix these issues, which can be frustrating when you thought you were outsourcing maintenance to the LLM.

articles.title: Successful Companies Go Blind
articles.summary: Successful companies go blind when they achieve success, a phenomenon similar to how the Mexican cavefish loses its eye genes after millions of years without them. This happens because once a company has reached a certain level of success, it stops recognizing what it took to get there and starts prioritizing comfort over competence. As a result, engineers who have never worked elsewhere may not suspect anything is off, but from within the company's perspective, life inside the cave is unflattering.

articles.title: Late Bronze Age Collapse
articles.summary: The Late Bronze Age Collapse, a period commonly abbreviated as the "LBAC" or "Late Bronze Age Collapse", refers to the collapse of the Late Bronze Age state system across the Eastern Mediterranean and Middle East during the 12th century BC. This event is significant because it marks a turning point in the broader Mediterranean world, potentially leading to a more severe decline than the Roman Empire's collapse. Archaeological evidence plays a crucial role in understanding this period, as it provides valuable insights into what was happening at the time but often struggles with precision and causality.

articles.title: Good Tools Are Invisible
articles.summary: A good tool should strive to make itself invisible, not just by hiding its shortcomings but also by making them seem like a fun puzzle to solve. This is particularly true when it comes to text editors like vim, which can be frustratingly limited in their ability to handle certain tasks. When you're proficient with your editor of choice and it lacks the tools you need, it becomes invisible, but treating that friction as the "fun" part can lead people to advertise it as evidence of its greatness. In contrast, a good tool should focus on making itself useful without drawing attention to its limitations.

articles.title: Cpp2Rust: Automatic Translation of C++ to Safe Rust
articles.summary: Cpp2Rust translates C++ to fully safe Rust automatically, using clang's AST as a syntax-driven translator. It first parses the input file(s), traverses the AST, and emits Rust code as strings, inserting calls to libcc2rs for raw pointer semantics. The generated code is then pretty-printed using rustfmt to produce a single .rs file with reference counting safety.
